speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: With my memory, my childhood memories of some of the things, the way they did them, as I remember.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Is that all right?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: For example, I remember my aunt during the wet years, the hard years, 1885, 1883 to 1985, why she had, they couldn't afford to buy candles, or they couldn't get them if they could afford them. And then they made their candles for the Christmas tree. She made them on a towel, beef towel, because they had quite a bit of beef towel stored up, and she'd melt that towel and made an improvised place to improvise really an old oil can, a five gallon oil can, with a rod over it, a wooden rod across the top, or two or three, I think, she'd have some kind of, over the can. And she could put strings, regular grocery strings, maybe she'd make them heavier, so they'd be four or five ply or six ply or something like that, that's the way I remembered. And she'd lay those across this little rod, and then she'd dip them down in the melted towel, maybe half a dozen at a time, and dip them down in the towel, then she'd set it up so they'd cool and drip, see. And then when they were cooled, well then she'd start all over again, she'd work with half maybe a dozen strings on little rods, and on little wooden rods, and then she'd dip them and dip them, and finally she just kept dipping them and cooling them until they were candles. Then when she got them completed her task, well then she snipped the threaded B double or triple, why she'd snip the top of it, and that would be the wig, see. And that's the way she made candles, she made little ones for our Christmas tree, and then she made some big ones for the church, and bigger ones for the church, and that just meant she had to have longer thread and make a heavier cord out of it, see, just make it like a cotton cord, and that's the way she made candles. And I remember going to Sunday school, and during those lean years, and they had those candles in the windows, homemade candlesticks of some kind, I don't know what they were, I presume metal that some of the men made, and they'd put those candles in the window, about three in each window of the church, and they burned, they burned until they were burned, and that was what they used for Christmas candles. There were no electric lights, there were no electric candles, there weren't even any holders, they just made their holders, that was them. But I remember later on the farm, when we bought our candles, and had little spring holders, that we clamped on the Christmas, mulems of the Christmas tree. But I had the pleasure of remembering how they made homemade candles, and my father told about the ladies making homemade candles for their church, for this old church, 800 -year -old church, when he was the boy. And that's a true story.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did your family celebrate Christmas? Did you give Christmas presents? At home?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, I'll tell you this, I described it in the book, but I'll tell you this, because if you're taking Christmas with the big Scandinavian Christmas, and we came in our family, it was my mother's, my father's memory, I suppose as much as my mother's, because my mother was raised in this country really. But my father, Christmas was the big day of the year, and I described it in the book, but then they had always got their Christmas tree, and there was one gala day when the boys, the brothers, were small, and dad would go to our place in the Moscow Mountains, we had 40 acres of land, and they'd cut the Christmas tree and haul it home, and keep it outside until they were ready to decorate it the day before Christmas Eve, they had to have it all done, because Christmas Eve was the big day, not Christmas Day, but Christmas and that was a heritage that they had retained from the old country. And all, and I remember, as we grew up, and I was the oldest, so I remember my doll, that I got one nice big doll that I got for Christmas when I was maybe four or five years old, and that was the first disc doll that I got, disc, B -I -S -Q -E, that means, you know, that they made this one in porcelain, it was disc doll, the old porcelain dolls weren't as nice, but the first disc doll had artificial hair on it, and I got one when I was, I imagine four or five years old, because I walked to church that morning to the early services, and they called it in Scandinavian, and that was one of the few words that some of us kids knew, was Yule Ota, Yule Ota, which meant early church services, Yule is Christmas, and they, I had gone to church with my father at four or five years old, four years maybe only, and we walked to church to that Yule Ota, which was in the church and early, everybody came to church at five o 'clock in the morning, and had to be there five for this service, and then after services we walked home, and sometimes we're glad if it's a snow, we'd ride in a sleigh, but we often walk, and even some of the younger brothers remember of walking to that service, which they had done away with years and years ago, in fact the younger brothers and sisters don't remember that early service at all, they just done away with it, Americans are too lazy,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: and then we had Christmas Eve was the big time, and Christmas Eve, by that time we had our house all cleaned up, as clean as we could get it, if we had to have a room paper, we'd have the paper, the dining room paper, or room paper before Christmas, because that was going to be the day when everything was in absolute clean and in order, and then they had the house in order, they did the chores early on Christmas Eve, and then after Christmas, I mean after dinner, not after dinner, before dinner, they did their chores, well we had our Christmas dinner, Christmas Eve, and then we just had the butchering done, and the baking all done, and the homemade sausage, and put the sausages in brine, and for three weeks I believe, and then also some of the beef they put in brine, which they later made chip beef out of, and then they had fresh meat too, because it was cold enough so they could keep the fresh meat for over Christmas usually, and they'd have roast or fresh pork chops, or anything like that, and then they nearly always had a roast goose for Christmas, a roast goose, and we waste geese at home, and so they always planned to have a geese plucked and take them to market at least a week before Christmas, so they could sell them, and then we always had one roast goose for home, for our Christmas dinner, but that didn't mean that we just had roast goose, we'd cook some of these sausages, and were they ever good, and had them hot sausage, maybe we'd have some cold ham

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: for home cured ham, and then always the roast goose and the roast goose was stuffed with dressing just like real good old -fashioned bread dressing, which we liked, and roast goose gravy, and mother tried to skin some of the fat off of it, because our geese were real fat, and then she'd always feed it, we had brown gravy, and then we had, we didn't make plum pudding very much, but we had all kinds of baked goods like rolls, rusks, and cookies, mother made two five -gallon oil cans, we got kerosene in those that we used for our lamps, and then we'd scrub and clean with lye water, and scrub and clean so they were absolutely clean, and then they used those for cookie containers, and before Christmas my mother would make a five -gallon can of ginger snaps, and she made them large, not little dany ones, we liked the larger the better, dany ones, ginger snaps, and white sugar cookies, and of course we never had very many eggs during Christmas, because that was when the hens had their vacation, and but we, she'd try to save up some eggs for the cakes and things like that, and most everybody knew shortcuts, making food cake without eggs and things like that, and my mother made very very good candy, made bonbons, and she made real fondant, she learned how to make real fondant, and she used the top of the commode, the slab of marble on top of the commode, she used that to make her, to mix her fondant on, and make the little chocolate creams and coconut greens and all kinds of nice candy, which was, I say it was just about, her fondant was just about as nice as they make it nowadays, in the candy shops, and then she dipped her chocolates, and she decorated her coconut creams and different kinds of candy, and she always made homemade peanut butter in a great big iron skillet, she'd take pure cream, and for her, let's see, she'd take pure cream and sugar, brown sugar, I believe, I've forgotten, I think it's brown sugar, she, no, I think she, she used sugar, either brown sugar or white sugar, and I've forgotten, and then when it got to a certain consistency, she had, she put that cream in it, after it was kind of melted and browned a little bit, or then she put that cream in it, and stir it up, and put the peanuts in, the raw peanuts in there, and stir that on, in that big skillet until it was, the right consistency to pour out on a big platter, we always have big platters on the farm, and she'd pour, pour that on a great big platter, and then when it cooled, why then, then she um cut it into, broke it into, into peanut butter, and she'd have a great big box full of, shoe box full, or a big container of peanut butter, so we, we were lucky, we had nice homemade candy, and um, sounds fantastic, that meal, oh, and oh, the wasn't, we had vegetables, we had to use mostly vegetables, but one, two kinds of vegetables that they used to use for Christmas, and when they had their, their big dinners during the holidays, where they'd invite the relatives, or neighbors or friends, to come over for a meal, and there was always, it was always similar to this Christmas, but the Christmas dinner was our family dinner, and, and my grandpa, grandfather and grandmother, and one uncle, who wasn't much older than I was, they'd always come out and bring a sack of, um, of gifts, and candy, and oranges, and always two coconuts, in the shell, and they'd bring, come out on Christmas, the grandpa, or they, they, they used to walk up some time, but most always, why, grandpa, they hitch up the horses, and sleigh bells, and go in and get grandpa and grandma to come out, but we, it was always just our family, like just today, our family dinner, and, and, and the other dinners that I'm talking about were anywhere between Christmas and New Year, or, or 10 days later, and, um.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you celebrate New Year's at all?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh yeah, you met, but not like that, but Christmas is what the kids all remember, and they, and they included, and it was Christmas, and Christmas was both sacred and, and, uh, enjoyable, it was, uh, it was, the gala day of the year, and, um, and then we, uh, after the men had their chores done, well we tried to have, mother tried to have the dinner already, and that meant that we had a nice stretched out our table, full length, and, um, had a nice linen tablecloth, pure linen tablecloth, that was a gift from my mother, my grandmother. My grandmother always gave my mother a three yard tablecloth, and real linen, and she'd hem it by hand, and most of the time she'd do some handwork, or drawing work in it, or all around the whole edge, and we had our napkins, large linen napkins, 18 inch, or 24, I've forgotten which, I rather think they were 24 inch squares, of real linen, that's my grandma gave her, and nothing was too good for Christmas, we used that linen, and used those napkins for our Christmas dinner, and then we had our Christmas tree lit, but maybe we wouldn't light it until after we were through with dinner, because the candles were at premium, and by the time that there were several children, none of them remember of seeing homemade candles made, that was bygone days when they had such a hard time that, I remember my dad telling about, um, they had got five dollars for Christmas, you know, I don't remember where they said they got it, I suppose sold a hogs, or half a dozen hogs, or something like that, or a cow, or something like that for Christmas, maybe, or sold geese, and got that five dollars, but they got five dollars a piece, my uncle Andrew got five, and my dad got five, he made them, they went to town, and they bought a dollar's worth of sugar, they couldn't buy any gifts, all the gifts we had then were homemade, this was really before I remember, because, see I was only three years old, but, um, and then they bought this, each one of the men bought a pair of overalls, we call them jeans maybe now, but they were overalls, blue overalls, and that, they bought them for Christmas, and of course they had to use them for every day, and I suppose they had their old wedding suit or something that they were still using for church, for good, and, um, and then, um, the rest of that five dollars, they bought a can, let's see, they bought each, a dollar's worth of sugar, and they're overalls, and then they had the rest of their money, they, they bought a can of kerosene for the lance, and, um, I think coffee, some coffee, that was the Christmas during the, what they called the hard years, the wet years, and that was 1883 to 1885, those two Christmases, but they, they're just wanting any money for anybody, not for the farmers, and then, uh, might have been a little bit for the college professors, but they only had about a half a dozen of them, a dozen of them at most, that they had up here at the university, because the university was, uh, was started the year that I was born, and the, so the birthday of the state, my birthday was the same, 1890, uh, yeah, well, now, going on, going back to the gifts and the Christmas presents, later, in the later years, when I was four or five years old, I got my doll, when I was about three or four, before maybe, and then every, well, all the children got one gift from dad and mother, one gift, and that usually wasn't clothes, it was usually one gift, one toy or one gift, and one year I got the doll, the next year I got a cradle, a little white cradle, and my mother made two little sheets, she put lace on one for the top sheet, she made two little pillowcases by hand, a lot of lemons of pillow taking, and feathers, we always have plenty feathers at home, she made her own, own, um, pillow, she always made her own pillows, and stuffed them with the feathers that she saved from, from the goose down and from the chicken feathers, some of the, some of the boys got chicken feather pillows, and for the best pillows, where she used her goose down, and, um, but she always made them, and, um, then for this cradle, she made a little quill, I remember it was red calico, red, and it had a lining, and, um, and then she made, she had a little blanket, a piece of blanket, of an old, old blanket, wool blanket, and she took her back and put a little blanket on it, with a little blanket, that she put on in that cradle, under the, under the sheet, and we didn't, we didn't have a bedspread, we just had the quilt, the red quilt, that's the way I remembered, and I put my dog to bed in that, undress that doll, she always made socks for it, she made, she made, um, dresses for it, I took my doll, that same doll, to school when I was in third grade, and I kept it so nice, but mother made a new dress for it, and she made that dress, I imagine I had had a dress for that material, but it, I remember that it was blue, a real beautiful blue cashmere, and she made a little white vest out of wool, that she, made a sailor collar, upper jacket, a sailor, uh, with a sailor collar on like they were in those days, the girls wore them, I had some to school, for school, and, um, and then it had a skirt, and I took that doll to school, to have it for a program, on the last day of school, one, one time, and, uh, I, I was so proud of my doll, because mother made all these clothes, and, uh, they had, the, the doll had her little shoes, shoes, her shoes that she wore when she purchased the doll, but, um, so that each one of us got, but grandpa and grandma always brought one more gift, sometimes they'd have a little extra, something that grandma made, or like she'd give mother some dish towels, or besides her linen tablecloth, but she wouldn't give that every year, but she'd give maybe a homemade rubs, that my grandmother wore on her loom, and she collected rags from my mother out of old, uh, men, some of the men that worked there, had leave wool clothes, or wool overcoaster, or clothes, and she, and she used, uh, some cotton, uh, dresses, or new material, for, and she wove real nice looking rugs, and that's what we used upstairs in our bedrooms, were grandmother's homemade rugs, she even made a rug for the stairway one time later, later, and, uh, and, uh, a narrow rug that she wove on her loom, and gave us, because my grandmother only, we were the only grandchildren, children that my grandmother, my maternal grandfather and grandmother had, and they lived in Moscow, and, uh, now, now that, now our Christmas, after our dinner, while we all, our house is always warm, we had just heating stoves, and cooked stoves, and we opened the parlor door, or living room door, which we didn't have open all the time, because the parlor had to look nice, for a company, and our bedroom was never used by the family, except in, in emergencies, or when we had guests, so then we used to, that's your downstairs bedroom, downstairs bedroom, yeah, but, um, but grandpa and grandma, they, they would, they wouldn't stay overnight, they'd take them back in the sleigh, or the hack, or something, they never stayed overnight, because they had milk, cows to milk at home, and they wanted to be home in the morning, and, uh, so they always took them home, and that Christmas, we celebrated that Christmas about as long as we were on the farm, and finally I had seven brothers and two sisters, and, and, and grandpa and grandma had moved away, but, but we still celebrated our Christmas the same way, and some of the children were married, and they came home with their children, and we made room for them to sleep on the floor, or on the dam, or on the sofa, and anywhere, we just, even in our old antique bed, some of the younger children had sleep four or five crossways in that bed on Christmas Eve, when we had, uh, uh, had all the family there. That was some years later, but that was while we were still all home, and, and, uh, we're at home still with, uh, with together, and then our, our gift time was around the Christmas tree, and we never had a real Santa Claus, but one of the older brothers would always act at Santa Claus, and we'd distribute the gifts after, and, and then we had this dinner, of course, if we wanted some snacks, we could always go and get a cookie, or get some, and the oranges and the nuts that the grandpa brought, and the coconuts and that, we all had to have, have to, have those, and so by Christmas morning, early Christmas morning, our house was anything but an order, but we had, we'd have breakfast, and we'd fix breakfast, maybe have cereal, and always had some of the cold meat, and, um, you know, the whole table full of, of food that was, that we used for breakfast, and then we'd finally clear it off before dinner on Sunday, or on Christmas Day, rather. Then, maybe we'd have a roast, or, or roast, uh, chickens, or some, some of the roasts, and have another dinner, but it was more, uh, like our ordinary family Sunday dinners the next day, and then we had, we had plenty leftovers, baked stuff, and things like that. We always had a lot of food in the cellar, low, and we wanted to. That was another custom we had every night when the kids did their homework around the dining room table, the old, old, dining room table, why, we'd, uh, somebody go and bring out a pan of apples for, to eat, we'd just, about, eat two and three apples apiece, and, uh, there was never any limit. We always were, uh, not to eat as many as we wanted to, not waste any, though.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: No, what? That's Christmas? What could be better than that?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I tell you, that was what made our, our family and families of the olden times close together, and to this day when I finished my book and told about our childhood, the brothers had repeatedly written, thank you and thank you, over again, and how we remember our childhood, and that's true, that's the gospel, too. This brother Ted in Las Vegas, he, he's retired now from his, uh, he was, uh, he was from the high school kids, and they just love it.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Why are they, why are they loving it?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: They're loving it because it, it's, it's real life, and they just miss something, they don't know what they missed, and they just love to, to, to, and those, I, I say these high school kids are so interesting to work with, and they are thinking, and, and I think that, that, well, Watergate or whatever comes in our way, it's not, it's not going to be, it's going to be different. People have, it's brought us to think, I, I just, I think that, and I, I could be out in a nursing home with those people. I'm a lot older than a lot of them that I cater to at the hotel, and I just feel like we just have to keep up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What's some of the stores in Moscow that you remember, you know, and what they were like?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I remember the first one. Uh, you think, I mean the ones that you remember yourself as a child, you know, going into. Yeah, I remember, I remember David's store. It was, it wasn't David's store, it was Kauffman. It was, it was, what do you mean, Kauffman,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Durnham, Durnham and Kauffman store, and they were, they were, they were Jewish people that came from the east, and they came here and bought that store. It had begun in a seat.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, they, they, I guess they built it. And what I remember about, my mother says I can't remember but I, I know I remember that store, and that was when Kauffman, Durnham and Kauffman, they were Jews, and they later sold it to David, see, David Neely, he yelled why. Eley was the brother -in -law of, of Mr. David, Frank David, and that was Homer and, and Earl David's father, and the David boys. They were four of them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were those, were those people accepted, even though they were Jewish?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh yeah, they were, they were well thought of. Oh yeah. In fact, I, I just don't think that we made any discrimination. There was a little, a dorky girl, a necro girl, in, in my first grade class, and her husband, her father rather, owned a little restaurant. I don't even remember where he owned the restaurant, but it's on Main Street somewhere, and I think it's just a little one. I don't know where it was, and I haven't found anybody home where David didn't know, and I don't, there's nobody here that I know, would know, because there's nobody here. I don't think there are very many people here older than I am.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And, and, and that lady, that girl lived down on this Lynn Avenue, this is Lynn Avenue, and it goes down over the hill, and right at the brow of the hill, farther down, not very far from White Avenue, you know. We, I, it bothers me to have, have them call that White Avenue, because to us, it was all scottable lanes. We always called that scottable lane, and, and in my book I write about scottable lanes, and it should be scottable lane, because scottable owned that whole strip of land where the automobiles are now, and automobile places, the Ford and those railers.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember the, the name of that black girl who was in your class?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, I do. Mary McCrae. Mary McCrae, N .C. C -R -E -A -G -I -B -E. C -R -E -A McCrae. And she, she, we, all of us played with her. We never, she had a little black pig tail here, a little one here, and she was always well dressed, and she was in school, but we, I can't remember that we made any distinction. Well, I don't think the kids nowadays do as much as their parents. It's all these mothers that are so afraid of their kids playing with Negro kids, because I realize it's different in the East and the South. I've been in the South, my brother, one brother owns a jewelry store in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, and I've been down there twice. I stopped there when I came back from Europe in 53, and I've been there, I was there in 66, I think. So I've been there, he and there, the French girl down there, and her, this French girl.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there any Chinese that you remember?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, that I remember. There were quite a few Chinese here, and there, and another thing I remember, they, they had, you know, in Jackson Street, on the other side of Jackson Street West, right along there were several old shacky looking places, you know, and there was a Chinese laundry, maybe two Chinese launders. I believe one was up North Main Street, though, but we remember the Chinese people. In fact, what I remember is the old Moscow Hotel, which was called the Barton House, was located where Moscow Hotel is now, and this old Barton House was a framed building. I've got a picture of it, but a framed building, and they,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: it burnt down that I think the day, same day, or the next day, when my grandmother and grandfather came here, these maternal grandparents, they came here, and mother was 13 years old, and her brother was three months old, three months old, when they came to Idaho, and they came from Minnesota, but they came here because they knew some, the rants did, some people here that had lived back there, and that same, near the same place in Minnesota, so they knew them, and so that's why they came to Idaho, I think, and grandpa bought the first house, his first home, right down here on the other side of the street, right down the corner, 7th, and Lynn, right down here, and when I bought this house, I never thought about it, never even remembered it. It was Lynn Avenue, but what I learned was, not then, I knew it long before that because my mother knew it, but when my grandpa came here in 1886, or 87, my dad came in one year, and they came in the other year, and I don't know which one came, and dad didn't know my mother, he'd never heard of her people, but he came here because he had the contract to build this railroad, to excavate for the railroad, and his contract was from Spokane Falls, I told that lady this today, Spokane Falls, down to Julietta.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Can you tell me the story again about that homestead they had near Walla Walla, the homestead that your father and his brother had? Oh, near Pasco, wasn't it? Didn't your father have a, and his brother took up a homestead near Pasco?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, yeah, that's right, that's at Pasco, see that's why they used their homestead rights, and it was, they called it the Horse Heaven Country, and when we had gone over to the coast, I went with my parents many times, and I went with my brothers, and this white, whitey who lived with me, he knew just exactly where it was, and I couldn't tell you exactly, but it was very close to Pasco, I think north of Pasco, well I don't know where it's north, the Pasco is right on the Columbia River, I think, I believe it is, well it was close to the Columbia River, because my father used to tell about, they came from Colorado, he and his brother, and they and two other men with him, came with him, came together from Colorado, they worked in the Leadville Iron and Silver Mine, see in Colorado, they lived there quite a bit, but dad didn't want to be a miner, he just, they wanted to get a farm, well they heard about the homesteads that they had in Washington, and that was the territory of Washington, see Oregon, Washington, and Idaho were three, were all together in one territory, and then I believe Oregon became a state before, I believe that's right, and but dad in Washington, they they were selling or giving away homesteads, see, and so they went there and got this homestead, and I suppose they got, it was near Pasco, and I suppose they'd already taken a lot of the best ones, you know, farther, I don't know whether they had them on, but they were, the coast was still, still it was lead territory, but the coast was settled, see, and see it was about 1826 that Lewis and Clark, I believe, came here, and settled in Washington, they didn't settle in Washington, but explored it.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well how come your father, they couldn't make a go of that homestead?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Goal, now that's just exactly the question some of those high school kids had, well listen, when they came to there they took up this homestead, and I suppose they'd spoken for it, I don't know where they'd gone to speak for it, Walla Walla I suppose, Walla Walla and Madie, well then they went there and they bought some equipment, they bought some walking piles and a seeder and and we're going to start farming, see, and they got out there what there was hundreds of acres, hundreds of acres, thousands maybe, many thousand, along the Columbia River north of the Columbia River, Pasco was nothing but a railroad station, and the land there was nothing but sand, sand, sand, and they could, they plowed that sand, there wasn't even water, they couldn't, they didn't have any way of getting water out of the Columbia River, they couldn't pump it, it wasn't that close, but he said we went, I remember, he said there was an Indian squall, they used to ferry or take some of these homesteaders across the river, and I don't know what they went across for, but they, she'd take them across and see, she gave them horses, and each one got on the horse, and the horses would swim across the Columbia River and with the men on the back, see one on each horse, and so they'd go across there, and she gave him two or three pieces of gold, gold nuggets, and I never did, he never, he never did explain a lot of things, but evidently she had gotten these from different ones that had been down in California in the Gold Rush days, and she had gotten these for money or for pay from some of these people, well she got quite well acquainted with Dad, and Dad had a nice disposition, and he'd be friendly to an Indian or an Eagle or whatever it was, didn't want to make him different, he's just that kind of a man, I guess I, thank goodness that I think I got a lot of my father, because I just love people, well, and so they didn't, they got this whole land, well when they tried to plow this and put seed in there, and no water, no water came, no water, no water, well then they realized they knew that much about farming, because they had a farm in the old country, and the good one too, raised good crops, and so they decided they're not, that wasn't for them, they would, they never make a living even unless they got out of work, so he said we buried our machinery and our equipment and our boots, he said, in the sandpit, and then we took our coyotes, he called them, and rode horseback to Spokane Falls, because he'd heard that they were building a new railroad there, and they had the Oregon, Oregon N, O, R, N, railroad was built to Portland, I think, from Walla Walla to Portland, I think, but the Northern Pacific wasn't in yet, I don't believe it was in nor area, no, I don't, it wasn't here at all, I don't think, and so the Pacific was going to bring, or build this line from Spokane down to Juliaetta, and so the man who was doing this excavating and had that contract, he got sick, or his family got sick, or something he had to quit, and he said, Nelson, he said, you take this contract, and my dad, why he couldn't even speak, hardly speak English, he had been in this country though nine years, eight or nine years, and come across, he worked in Chicago, and he worked in the Pennsylvania, and then he went, went almost westward to see, and so then they came up to Spokane, and and when he told him that, he said, I can't even talk yet, he said, how can you expect me to take care of the crew, and he had the equipment and everything, the tents, and the mules, and the whole setup, and the, some of the crew, you know, and so they decided to come down to, or he took it, he decided to take the contract, and he finished it too, he went clear down to Juliaetta, he couldn't go into Spokane, because, or into Lewiston rather, because the Indian reservation had just been created, Nez Perce Indian reservation, and they weren't, that was against the rule for him to bring a railroad in, in, in the, within the bonds of the Nez Perce reservation, so that ended his, his, his railroad.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did he excavate the, with mules and scrapers?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: What's a scraper, because you mentioned that last time. Hand scrapers, and then they held those, and that had just loathed them, and that, that, rather, this farm out here, by our farm, see, like this, this is our farm, it's supposed to be, that's the painting, and doesn't look much like the farm, but, and on the other side of that border there was another farm, and that was the Northrop farm, and that Northrop farm was another homesteader, see, and that our farm was taken up by the elements, John and I got their, I showed them to you, and they took that farm, and where the house is now, was the, where the barn, log barn was, and east of the house there, over a waste, she hasn't got that, you can't even tell what's supposed to be, my brother thinks that's terrible, that I paid money to have that painting, well she, she made a good painting out of it, she's, I know, goodness, my dad had a cooking tent, and he had a tent for the men to sleep in, and he had to have the big tent, you know, and I remember the tent, they used it on the farm for a long time, and all, all that equipment they had, oh, I, we, I can remember a pancake griddle that mother used, she, I think she had two or three of them, and, and they had, they'd take the, the stove lids off, you know, there was two stove lids, and they were held up by a removable piece in the middle there, and then they'd just take that pancake griddle and put it right down in there, and when the wood burned down kind of low, so there was coals, well they'd put that pancake griddle, she made pancakes for the whole family, and that many a time, I, we, all, and most of us can remember that, I'm imagining my younger sister remember when mother made, well, we, I don't know, but we had, no, we didn't have electricity when she, I think we got electricity in 1919 on the farm, but we got it a lot earlier than the rural electrification because we were so close to the, the cemetery, and they brought the electricity out the cemetery from town.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were there any, uh, who, who composed the crew that built the railroad? Well, see, see there was, there was a big company, like, uh, what's his name, that Hill?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Hill, he, he, I think he was the president of a, of a company that, built the northern civic, and see there was railroads all over the United States. I meant just the crew that your father, oh, like my father, he had to do all that, see, and he got, he got this equipment, and then he, he got the contract, and they paid him so much for the contract, I don't know how they paid him if they paid him, but the money and everything that he got, he, they gave him so much for the contract, see, and whether they gave it to him at one time or not, I don't know, but I know he did, the, the first bank that dad did business with was, was the building that was where the, uh, first national, no, yeah, first national bank is, now, that was the wooden building there, and that was Baker's bank, and he, Mr.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Baker, mayor, became, went to Walla Walla, but dad, when he had the camp, dad had the camp out here by the foothills of the, I told you about the foothills of the Comer -Spute, where that, you know, where that place had, had, uh, buffaloes, you know, that, that was the old, that place, that house was, was there when my dad had his camp there, and he had his camp right on that barnyard there, where those, where that house is, and, uh, I think there were, there was a family that had taken, taken it up and homestead, see, but they, that, that man died, let's see, no, I'll tell you how it was, the Indians killed him, the Indians killed that man, and, um

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know why?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Why, no, the, the Indians didn't ask anybody why they went, wanted to kill the wife, they wanted to kill him, that's all, and they, they had her rolled by the cemetery, which, which you go out now, only that, what I'll tell you right now, before I forget it, is that the road, the old road, went scat -a -bow lane, white -sabot, and turned up past the cemetery, or up, they weren't in the cemetery when, when mother and dad came here, or when they came here, there was no cemetery, that was part of the school section, that cemetery was, and the other part of it was across the railroad track there, but see the, the, um, the railroad had to buy right of way through the school section land there, at that corner, and, um, and, and, uh, but when I was from Says that, that, um, these, this Chapman place, it was Chapman, but there had been another man, Chapman was the man she married, but I don't know what her name was before, but this woman told my mother this story now, so this firsthand story, and, because mother used to go out there before she's married, my dad would take her out to the camp, to shore the railroad camp, and it was, it was a big project, you know, they thought of, they seemed like big, I don't know, I suppose he had maybe 25 or 30 scrapers, you know, and, uh, and mules, mostly mules, that's because he had one horse, he called him Jim, and he used to ride horseback on that Jim, and when he was working down at Kendrick, it was almost down at Kendrick, down at the bottom of the grade, why, my, my, they were married then, they had gotten married before he, well, yeah, they'd gotten married just before he quit the contract, um, maybe he was just mopping enough, I don't know, but anyhow, he, she wanted him to come home to go to a wedding of a friend that they'd met here in Moscow, I don't think they'd known them, they'd known them since they came to Moscow, and, um, no, there weren't hardly any young women, you know, a lot of railroaders, a lot of people here that stayed in Moscow, and, and, um, uh, farmers, but, but there, there weren't hardly any young women here, and so that's why my mother, my mother said, well, he said that's why all these young women got married, because there were, every one of them had about a half a dozen suitors, and that includes me, that includes me, and, uh, and so we used to question her, who she went with, and who she would deal with, and all this, and that, some of them we knew, and some of them we didn't. But what I, what I was gonna say is that, um, what was I gonna tell you?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: About Jim?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh yeah, about Jim. So she told him that she wanted to go at that wedding, and all right, he said I'll come home, and he took, he, he took Jim along, he's a, a horse, and, um, he didn't, they didn't even have this house built, or I, I imagine that they'd bought the land, because they bought that before he, they were married, from the homesteaders, from the dealmen, you see, but they lived down in this north place, but they didn't live in the log cabin, they didn't think that it was kinda, well these people had just certain, you know how long it took for rabbits, and monkeys, and, and everybody to run through there, and pigs, and everything else, I suppose, and so they, uh, they'd left, and, um, and they built a, a new little home, or it was just one room up, big room upstairs, and one downstairs, and, um, well two, they divided into kind of a living room, and a kitchen, I, and downstairs, and they had a pantry under the stairway, and the stairway was in the middle of the room, and then there was, I think, just one open place upstairs, where they slept, and, um, and he bought this bedroom set, about that time, about 1886, you know, from, from, uh, from this homesteader that was up on the Paradise Mountain, and he was gonna leave too, see, and he wanted to get rid of the bedroom set, so Dad said, well I'll buy it, I don't know, I wish I knew how much he paid for it, I bet he didn't pay 20 dollars for it, and uh, so what, wait, let's go back to the story about the horse, but Jimmy, but Jim, then he took the horse and rode down to Kendrick, down the old grade, not the highway that's there now, and they, they were working, some of the men were working down there, because they still, I think they were moving their, their tent or moving their equipment, anyhow, moving it back, they moved it back to the farm, see, here, and, and they had two great big barns on that North Hill place, great big barn, they were just gonna have a lot of hay, you know, and have a lot of cattle, well, and so Dad rented that land because there was a lot of room for his mules, and a lot of hay and everything there, so he rented it, I don't know what he paid for it, and uh, but Jim, he took Jim down there, and he was gonna ride him home, while my, when Jim got down there, he didn't want to be there, and he got loose, and he went home, and so Dad said, well, I couldn't disappoint Mother, I walked home from, from Don and Kendrick, or Juliaetta, which is, I don't know which, which, their camp, but they, they didn't have a camp down there, they had their camp, I don't think they had their camp down there, because they were almost through, they worked out of out of this camp here, I think, I'm not sure, but I think they did, I think they worked out of the camp, and then they had their tents and that down there for the horses, and for the, for the men to sleep in, and things like that, I know, I suppose the horses were outside, it's kind of warm down there now, and, and he, and, and this horse walked, ran home, and Dad walked home, and I don't know they got another horse to ride back, or not, I suppose he did, and, but Mother told that about how we thought that was so funny, when she told about how Dad walked home to the wedding, from Kendrick, mind you, or Juliaetta, of course he walked over the hill first of all, but even then it's a long walk. Well, those are the good old days, I guess.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: One more thing, what was, do you remember the story that you was, almost started to tell about Mrs. Chapman?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, yeah, but the, but the killing, and yeah, she told that, because Mother went out there, she said she knew her quite well, and she and her husband, but I don't, I don't, if Mother knew his name, my daughter, I don't think she did, but it was her former husband, and they had a wagon they called, you know, like a spring wagon they'd call it, I think that was, not might have been an improvised homemade wagon top, but it was, it was a wagon anyhow, and their horses, and they were driving, coming from Moscow, and this was their little shopping center, you know, they just had a few, well, I, I tell you, I think they had that store down there by the Mountain View Road, I think that was where they had been, no, but she said that right in front of the cemetery, but I, I, I, she said that later, maybe, maybe she pointed out to her where the location was, and at that time there wasn't any cemetery, I don't think, when she, when this happened to her husband, but she was telling my mother about this, and she pointed out where the location was, and she said it's right where the cemetery is now, see, and she, they were driving home, and both they came from that store and go there and roll out east, and there came a band of Indians, Indians with bulls and arrows, and they had, and they saw this man and woman driving, and they attacked him, and, and shot at him with the bow and arrow, and they killed her husband right there in the wagon, and the women had to be just about as brave as any man, you know, and she said, I grabbed the lions and drove home with his body slumped in the wagon, and drove home as fast as she could, and she said they didn't, they didn't shoot me, they didn't shoot me, but if they wanted to, they could have shot her too, you know, but it just happened that she wasn't shot, and she was, she drove home to, to that, I don't, I suppose, I don't,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: three weeks, and that's in a schoolhouse that was right down there, and, you know, that's another road that runs parallel with that road where you go out,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: that is farther over, you can see it, you know, well, that's the road that I went, and it had rail fences on both sides of that road, that rail fence, and I went to school, and my cousin lived with me, this uncle's son, and he was four months older than I was or five, and we walked to school down that road between the rail fence, and when we got down to where there, where there's another road, you know, there's forks that goes from, from your highway out towards the mountain, Moscow, or Paradise Mountains, well, that was the road that

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I went to, and these Hamilton kids lived there, it's just about where the, I know where you're talking about, yeah, it's just about there, it's about where that Mobile Home Place is, that's where the Hamilton, the old Hamilton place, it's a little bit north of that, I think, right north of those Mobile Homes, and they were mean kids, and they'd, they'd kind of hide in, in these, you know, how the rail tents were zigzag, and they'd go in those places, and then there were rows, a lot of rows bushes, wild rows bushes, and they'd hide behind those rows bushes in those little places, and then when we came along, came to go to school, why, they'd steal our lunch, and we'd have a real lunch pail, you know, well, I don't, I don't, I think it was a lunch pail with a lid on top of it, you know, how they used to have them for milk pails or something, I imagine my grandfather used them for milk pails, or maybe mother did, I don't know, but I don't think we had a real lunch pail, I don't believe that, and, and they just took our lunch pails, see, well, we were, I was so bashful, and my cousin was worse, and he was older, but I always had to be there, learned to be the one in the family that kind of looked after all the rest of them, and so I told the teacher that they stole our lunch pail, and I remember he gave us some of his sandwich that, that one day, I remember that, but, and then,

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: but we, we didn't go there, but three weeks, because my dad found out that our land was mostly in the city school district, so that we were, we were tired to go to school in town, so we did, that's a mailman already.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever see Indians when, did you ever see Indians?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh goodness, yes, my, there were a lot of Indians. When we went to, when we went to school down there, there'd be herds of Indians coming by there every day, and they'd go that road, see that road, North and South Road, and go over that Paradise Ridge, and that was just a dirt road, you know. My dad excavated that road that's there now, because he, he'd done, he'd done so much excavating that he was kind of an authority on that, and the county has asked him to do several road projects for the one, for example, he, he excavated this big hill out here on South Main, you know, it just, he dug up, and he, he dug that up with mules and scrapers.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now where are you talking about?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, see, when you go from Main Street down towards Lewiston, see, and over towards that tavern and that, or down south, on the high, Lewiston, 95, that's 95.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You mean the hill just south of town?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, just south of, and dad excavated that, see, it's got a big dump there, or a big point there yet on the west side, and they just took out the dirt on the left side, you know, not very long ago, about two years ago or something. There, it was a big hill there, cut a high hill, it's the highest, the highest point there, where the hill was, where they made the road up to the campus, you know, and up to Ridge Road in there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now all he used, all he, did he ever use dynamite or anything like that?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh my, yes, did he? I say so, he used a lot of dynamite, and he was, he was supposed to be a real dynamite authority, and a dynamite man. Let's see, they said to old Nelson was, was the one they, and just a lot of people called on to do their dynamiting, because that's dangerous, and I got a, I got a story in my book, and you want to read that someday. It's a true story about this, this brother here, Fred, and my other brother, the one in Las Vegas, he's next to him, and then the other, one of the young brothers, the one that's in the Air Force, he lives in Phoenix now, and they were not allowed to, they knew that the dynamite was in the blacksmith shop, but they were caution, and they were very obedient. Years and years that dynamite was there, years and years, was that hid up in the rafters of the blacksmith shop. They never there, go up there, because they knew that they were there, but Fred said, well we got brave one day, and I was pretty, getting pretty big then, and he was the biggest one of the four, you know, and we stole the dynamite, and we, and my, this brother Ted, the one that tells me about his boy of the day so much in that, why he, he got a piece of pipe, and I don't know how big the pipe was, of course it was about two or three inches in diameter, and he stuffed, they got the dynamite, and stuffed, put the, stick a dynamite in there, and I think they had black powder too, I think they put black powder in there, and then they put a fuse on there, they had sense enough to do that, and we had a granary, an old granary, a real granary, old -fashioned granary, oh, where they dipped the grain in the blue vitriol barrel and all that kind of stuff. I know about the early history of the farm now, I know that, and there's some details that I don't know, but if you want a story about that dynamite, you read that book, that story in the book.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Are you not going to tell it to me now?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh no, I don't want to tell you that, because I, I, I, so I, I wore a side of that thing, and I got Fred to tell it to me, and you make a notation of it, but you can read it just as, you get it as much as, get it better in the book than you, than I can tell it to you.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was that, the dip grain in blue vitriol?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, see, they used to always save grain in their granary for seed. They didn't buy the seeds like they do now. Now Jack, he's got 700 acres of land that he's farming, and he sells all his wheat, you know, and barley, and, and peas, or he doesn't have peas now, he doesn't, the peas got kind of bad, and the price went down, so he, he laid off peas for a few years, but, and they'd have to dip it in blue vitriol, that's copper sulfide, and they'd have water in there, in this barrel, and the barrel would be right outside the granary, and, and then they'd have a board on there that they could stand on, right on the edge of the, right by the barrel, or low stand or something, they could stand on, and then they'd take the sacks of wheat, or old I think they did with those two, I'm almost sure they did, wheat anyhow, I know, and dip the, well, the sacks down in the blue vitriol, and soak them in that just a little while, I don't think very long, I told you I wasn't a very good farmer, and I'm not, but so those details I never had to pay much attention to, but, and then they'd, they'd raise that sack, they'd raise that sack up, and, and dump it on a, on a board there, that they're kind of a, oh like a dish string, you know, and then lay it there until the water all run back in the, because they didn't want to waste that, that vitriol, and then when they, when, then they had the, they just piled those sacks on the, on the rack, and, and, and they'd grow it and keep it from growing, but it killed the weevils or, or insects in the, in the grain, see, and they just had to do that, otherwise they, they'd had too much wild oats, that are, are bugs in it, weevils. And that's what they'd use for seed the next time. Well, they just fixed that seed, they'd save the grain, and had their own grain, they wouldn't buy the grain like he does, he buys the seed, or he, he makes some provision to hold it out, but he doesn't do it, they treat it down the, down the mill, you see, for seed, they treat the seed, so it's clean seed, they call it, and they have to pay for that processing, of course, and then they just take it off, and then plant it, and that's the way they all do now, but that wasn't the way they used to do.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And, did your folks always have wheat when you remembered, did they always plant wheat?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, they, they, they, they almost did. In fact, there, there was a time when they didn't think they could grow anything with this lush grass here, and that's why those people started, but they never even dreamed of planting wheat or anything like that. I, I, I can't tell you what year, and, but it wasn't very many years, and I think by the time that my father farmed, I'm sure that's right, that they raised wheat, because my father had one of the very first fresh machines in this country, and he even, and it was just, first they had a kind of a horsepower thing, you know, they just pulled the machine with horses running around, but I think that was only one year, but then they got a steam engine, see, and that pulled the, the machinery and the separator, so that they harvested, and they used to get, harvest hand people from Troy, mostly, because see, they didn't have much grain, and maybe there was somebody up there that'd thrash a little field of them, but they didn't, it was mostly timber, you know, and they'd cleared the land, and that was awfully slow, and they got, they got a lot of that land for homesteads, you know, out there on Dreskal Ridge and all around there, Hawaii. Most of that land was, was taken up on homesteads even after I remember, and so I, I, I know that, and they'd come down and they'd bring their bunk and sleep in the barn, sleep in the straw stacks, wherever they were thrashing, and they'd thrash, and they had to thrash all of their grain, which was only one farm, 240 acres, but that's a pretty big farm, that was supposed to be a pretty big farm, see, 240 acres. There was a few people farther out that had 320 or something like that, but, but most of them, that was a pretty big farm, a lot of them had 160, you see, that's what they got in the homestead, but see, these had been two brothers that got that farm, evidently they sold 88 acres, and they, and out of that, almost from the beginning, I guess from the beginning, he had, maybe not the first year, two or three, but he had a cook house, and he fed the men, and he always, nearly always used to get women cooked. He had men sometimes, old CB Green, that owned the laundry here, down here, why he, he cooked there when he first came from, he came, he came from the old country too, but he came to Boston, he lived in Boston for quite a while, and then he came out west, and he just hadn't started this laundry yet, and I could tell you what those early launderers looked like. That was really something, they didn't have any power washers, you know, at all, they just, they just washed all, and they wore those white star shirts, you know, and they'd, they'd wash those shirts by hand in the tub, and they'd have two or three women rub on the board. Now that's first ones I remember, and I really knew some of those people, so, and one woman was a good friend, and man too, was a good friend of my grandpa and grandma, and they took me down to laundry a couple of times, and they had great big, then they got a washing machine, oh they thought that's wonderful, but I don't know how they pulled that washing machine unless it's a gasoline engine, but there, when I first remember, they just washed all those clothes, sheets and everything, and in tubs and rubbed them on, and made their own soap mostly. We made our soap on the farm too, when I was a kid, always made our soap, mother made my soap, not for, not for, we always bought soap for our face and hands, you know, hands open and that, but for washing we always had homemade soap.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did they iron in the laundry? Did they...?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Yeah, they ironed it with high hand irons, that they, they had a big stove, a great big old wood stove that they heated those irons on, and those women ironed those, and then they later got some kind of a little iron or some kind, I don't know how that operate, but I remember they, they started collars, because I remember when my father had shirts and mother do the shirts, but they bought collars, separate collars, you know, and washed and ironed them, and they were just glossy, because they were ironed so, so well, you know, but they, they didn't do that until, until they had this kind of, but on the farm for a long, long time, why, like that three -yard tablecloth that I told you about, linen, why we murdered the iron that, ironed it by hand, somebody made her a, I think a hired man that worked there, we used to always have a hired man until the boys were big enough, why he made her an iron board, you know, ironing board, and, and she just heated the irons on the stove, and I ironed with those kind of, I ironed with them, because I tell you, I had started to iron when I was about 10 or 11 years old, and I dined the shirts and starch, we made starch and, what'd you make starch out of?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Oh, mortar, one, after I remember, at first I guess they didn't even have cornstarch, but we, we cooked, we had cole starch, they call it, and it was really a cornstarch. It was cole starch, and they were in kind of lumps, and, and you'd, you'd buy them in, in packages kind of like cornstarch, you know, just, uh, about that kind of package, and you could buy them, and we'd buy them, and, and you'd take about, oh, I'd put a half a cup for our washing, and, and make boiled starch out of it. You just put, put in cold water, and then heated boiled water on the, in the tea kettle, and, and, um, poured it right in the kettle, stirred in the kettle, where you add your cold dissolved cornstarch, see, and, and then if it, if it wasn't clear, real clear, well, you'd stick it on the stove a while, and, and finish cooking it, until it got clear, see, and then you cool it, cool it with cold water, and of course we didn't have ice or anything, we just, mother used to have, we had a pump, and they had a plank that they could move, uh, move away, off of the platform of the pups, see, and, um, and when, when she was gonna sell, she sold butter, you know, we had the old homemade stern, and they sold butter, and made it in two, two pounds of the rolls, and put cheesecloth around them, and put them in the bucket, you know, if I was getting a box, nobody had a box, and, uh, and take it to, to town to sell the butter, and trade it for groceries.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And how would you put the starch on the clothes?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: The starch, well, then you, all they, and now like, my mother, she made shirts for all the boys when they were little, and, uh, even the big ones, she did the same thing, and she washed all their, their shirts, and, um, the boys, when they went to school, they, they had boughtin' shirts, the older ones, I remember that mostly, they, she made them, but she made them, always made it for the younger boys, and then when, when, uh, they had sale on, on material, like in January, Creighton's and that, why, she'd buy material, and, um, and, um, and then when she had a spare minute, or, and I mean a minute, or an hour, in, in the wintertime, why she'd make two or three of those shirts in the afternoon, you know, and put buttons on them, and have colors on them, she just made hundreds and thousands of those shirts, you know, and, uh, men, when we washed them, we'd have maybe 25 of those shirts in the, in the wash, and, uh, we'd wash them by hand, and then finally we, uh, when I first remember, but I, I'm thinkin' if I don't remember that we did that too long, uh, but I remember when she washed by hand, and I can remember that, and I did too some, but not too much, and, uh, then when later we got a gasoline, uh, a washing machine that would turn with a wheel, you know, and we had to do that.

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: My brother and I, we used to turn that wheel, turn, turn it, and we'd have to wash that about, oh, in a way, 20 minutes, 15 or 20 minutes, and, and turn that wheel around, and that made the, the clothes that were in the washing machine, and, um, and then, uh, uh, we'd, uh, have a ringer, see, a hand ringer, and we'd turn that ringer, and I can remember when we got up, my brother and I and my mother was sick, uh, uh, uh, one winter, and a sick lot, and, and, uh, we'd get up and do the washing before we went to school, and, uh, we'd fill the washing machine, the boiler, and put it on the stove with cold water, pump water, and put it on the stove, and, uh, then in the morning I'd get up at five o 'clock, and, um, and, uh, the, the fire was laid, and start the fire, and then the water would start to heat, and then my brother would get up, and, and we'd, uh, wash those clothes just that way with that wheel, turn that wheel, maybe we'd have 15 or 20, and then while he turned the wheel, while I'd, we'd have an extra tub and rinse them in, and pull them out of that suds, and, and rinse them in two tubs, see, we rinsed them, we rinsed them first, and then we'd put them in blowing water, and rinsed them a second time, and then we'd ring them, ring them, move our, um, our hand ringer, rubber ringer, you know, and, uh, then I'd go out and hang them up, and, and we'd get most of it done before we went to school, see, and, and then what we couldn't get done, maybe the last tale of it, my dad would have to do it, but we did have a hard girl a lot sometimes, but hardly ever right in the wintertime, but when it got to the day you had to go out and do the spring work, and that's why we, we nearly always had a hard girl, we had a hard girl in the springtime, we, or in the summertime, we wouldn't pay him one, maybe, oh, two and a half to three dollars a week, it's about what we paid the hard girl, but then we had the, we, we were considered the prosperous farmers, really, my dad had a thrash machine, and when he got through with, with thrashing the grain at home, why he'd pull his rig out to, nor south here, to all these German farmers near Genesee and Barn Creek and around in there, and, and they'd head their grain, and we did too for a while, but not too many, because our fields were smaller, and they had more land, and they'd get together, you know, and do their heading, and stacked grain in, in long stacks one by one, side by side, and they'd, sometimes they'd even cover them a little bit with, oh, with old sacks or, or some canvas or something, but, or even some boards, something, so, so if he didn't get out there before, it even snowed, why, why he'd, he'd thrash that grain, and, and I, I told that in the book, and, and I'd, I remember when I was real small, Dad had the thrash machine, and we'd, he'd go out to, and cook, thrash for those, take his coke out, and thrash for those Germans out there for maybe a whole month after he was through, it'd be, it'd be snow before he'd come back, and then he'd go out later, and collect the money, and then Harley and any of those farmers would write checks, because they didn't believe in the banks, they'd lost, lost their crops, you know, during the hard times, or wet times, so they, they nearly always saved, sold their money, and put their gold in, in tin cans, and buried, buried those tin cans, all behind the rock, or behind the barn, or some place where they knew it was, they never made any bones about, oh, Dad told that many times, they never, nobody ever seemed to, to steal that gold, and when, when they paid me, why, I'd talk, we'd talk, and have a good time, and he knew the men real well, and they'd say, oh, Gus, come on, and I'll give you your money, and they'd take the gold out of that cans, and, and pay him, and I think he did most of his figuring in the hands, and if it happened to be a little bit too, too much, that never happened, not if Dad did it, but if it wasn't quite enough, why, he never said anything about it, but my mother's the one that kept track of, of the wheat receipts, whenever a load that went to town, why, she'd, uh, see him go to town, and, and then when they came home at noon or night, why, and they'd say, well, you, you, so, you brought in so many loads today, is that right? Yeah, Dad said that's right, all right, here's the receipts, and he'd fork over the, the wheat receipts, and she kept them in a baking pot again, and that anything?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: And, um, and they, and, and I, I told in the book, and I, how Dad would go, hitch up the horses, and, and take the hat and go out and spend the whole day collecting, and then I remember when I was just a little girl, maybe I was three or four, four years old, I suppose, or I wouldn't remember that, and I'd sit in this big bed in Mother's room upstairs, in this bed, and, um, she'd put my nightie on, and I, I suppose I had maybe one brother or two brothers, but they were, were, we had a crib, a nice big crib, and, and, uh, one of them had sleep in that, and I don't, I think she kept the baby with her most of that, and they just, they, they just let that, why, I bet nearly all the farmers kept the baby, the new babies in, in the bed between them, I bet they did, but, um, we had a crib though, so yeah, we always had a crib, and, um, that's more than, now this is held over here, she was born lot, she's only in her fifties, early fifties, fifty -one or something like that, and she was raised on the farm that called it, she said, we never had a crib, we just had no range, we never had a crib, we just had, had the straw mattresses, and, well I said, I said, you don't, I said our farming never was like that, never was, we never, we never lived on that kind of farm, we always had plenty to eat, we had plenty to warm clothes, they used to buy as much as a thousand dollars worth of clothes in the fall, when they, when they, after harvest, and the gold came in, and of course I can remember when that, when they changed the gold, and, and, uh, started to write checks, and the dad, dad, most of the time I remember why dad wrote checks, you know, but I, I do remember this gold business, but then that one, that was too cumbersome for one thing, and, and too dangerous, and, as the time got civilized, they just didn't use it, but, um, what we, uh, well, it's sure, it's sure a lot different, but as I say, my sister, she's, uh, older than you are, but then, then she doesn't remember too much about anything like that, she remembers more than I, I thought she did though, like she remembers when we got our telephone, she remembers when we got electricity, now we had gas lights for a long time, and we made our own gas out there, the carbide lights, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How'd you make gas?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, you bought this, uh, carbide in, in cans and drums, and then dad built this house, then, right by the cellar, he built a concrete, uh, a little concrete, oh, it looked like a little hot house, you know, but it built right on the cellar, and then they, they had tanks, they had two tanks, and they put, um, they put one tank, or had water in one tank, and then they poured this concrete, or not concrete, but carbon carbide in a, in a container that was made for us, you know, they, these bark, it was a regular light set up, uh, some people that put the, the carbide lights in the house, or they, they run little pipes, you know, along the ceiling or someplace, and just like wires, electric wires, see.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So what happened when they poured the carbide in?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: Well, then, then, then that carbide dropped down in that water, and then it'd make gas, see, and then, then the gas was lighter than the water, and there, there was a vent, a pipe that was vented out of the, uh, top of that gas tank, see, and it, it, it compressed the gas in, in another, in another tank right by it, see. Well, then that gas would go in those little tiny pipes. Now, my sister, she, she remembers that, because she remembers when we got electricity, and we got electricity long before. She said, I think she said it was 1916, and she was about, um, she was born in January of 1912, so she was that big then when we got her in the spring, I remember that, and, um, and then, but it was real nice light, really light, it was really, all that gas light was real nice and good, we thought it was wonderful, you know, and you just turned it on with a little switch, not like that, but a little switch that you turned with.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Yeah, to light it with a match too, didn't you?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: No, you didn't, they had a pilot light sometimes, you didn't have to, my light, because we had to light something this evening, you didn't have to light a mask, you, you, there was a pilot light, I don't really know, I can't even remember how that worked, but, but, uh, I know we didn't light it with a match, and, uh, we didn't have a gas stove, we had our old electric, or, um, wood stove, no coal, yeah, wood stove, we had wood, because we had our own wood on, on, are we gonna eat?

speaker: Elsie Nelson
words: I think we ought to break, yeah. Yeah, I think we better eat, our casserole is dead and gone if we don't.
